summary:
Approximately 40% of adolescents have gingivitis with higher rates observed in teenage girls likely linked to hormone changes; thus, it’s important to address potential risk factors early.
Gingivitis affects about one-third of teenagers, particularly those going through pubertal hormonal transitions which may explain increased incidence seen predominantly in young women. To manage risks effectively, preventive measures should be prioritized starting at an early stage. The summary captures the core findings regarding the prevalence and gender-related trends associated with gingivitis in adolescence while emphasizing timely intervention as crucial. ```plaintext
About 40% of adolescents experience gingivitis, notably affecting teen girls due to hormonal changes; therefore, addressing prevention strategies early can help mitigate these issues.
Prevalence estimates show approximately one-third of teenagers develop gingivitis, often exacerbated by hormones during puberty, underscoring the importance of proactive dental care beginning in youth.
```
